nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater streams and pools associated with peat swamps; heavily shaded by forest canopy, with soft, acidic water stained brown with tannins from decomposing leaf litter

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on zooplankton and small invertebrates. In captivity, generally accepts high-quality micro foods such as crushed flakes, micro pellets, frozen or live baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, micro worms, and rotifers. Food must be very small due to their tiny mouths.

summary: Tiny blackwater nano fish from Borneo peat swamps, among the smallest aquarium fish at ~1.5–2 cm. Best kept in soft, acidic water in schools of 8+; bold and vibrant in a well-planted species-appropriate setup.
careNotes: One of the smallest aquarium fish, reaching only around 1.5–2 cm. Endemic to southwestern Borneo (South Kalimantan), where it inhabits ancient forest peat swamps with extremely soft, acidic blackwater. Water chemistry is critical: this species tends to be sensitive to high pH and hard water, so soft, acidic conditions are generally advised. A well-established, biologically mature tank is recommended before introduction. Dense planting (Java moss, floating plants), leaf litter, and driftwood help recreate the blackwater biotope and provide cover for these naturally timid fish. Low to moderate lighting is preferred. Juveniles appear pale and may take time to develop their characteristic scarlet coloration as they settle in. Though peaceful, their tiny size makes them unsuitable for general community tanks with larger or more boisterous fish. pH source disagreement noted: Aquarium Co-Op lists 6.0–8.0 while SeriouslyFish, FishkeepingWorld, and blackwater habitat ecology all point to 4.0–7.0 as the functional range; 4.0–7.0 used as more ecologically accurate for this peat-swamp species.
breedingNotes: Moderate difficulty. Continuous egg-scatterers with no parental care; adults will eat their own eggs, so a separate breeding tank with a fine mesh or Java moss floor is generally advised to protect eggs. In good conditions they spawn daily, with eggs hatching in approximately 48 hours. Fry are very small and require infusoria or paramecium initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms after about 7–10 days. Males tend to be smaller with brighter red coloration; females appear rounder when carrying eggs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy, native range, water parameters, and husbandry. Used as primary due to depth of scientific and husbandry detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check for diet, temperament, tankmates, and general care. pH listed as 6.0–8.0, which is broader and less ecologically accurate for this blackwater species than the 4.0–7.0 range confirmed by other sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-check for taxonomy, size, and water parameters. pH listed as 6.5–7.0 which appears conservative for a blackwater species; temperature and hardness broadly consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-check for lifespan (4–5 years), pH (4.0–7.0 confirmed), and breeding notes. kH listed as 3–12 dKH which seems high for a blackwater species; 0–2 used based on habitat ecology and SeriouslyFish data.

fish.breedingNotes: Continuous egg-scatterer; adults eat eggs. Breeding tank with mesh or fine moss recommended. Fry require microscopic food initially.
